summary: I grew up in Iraq, a country in the Middle East where I lived with my family. Life in Iraq was not really bad; I went to school and played soccer with my friends. I left Iraq on April 28, 2016, seeking better education and opportunities for myself and my family. I brought my culture, traditions, and language with me when I left, but I also left behind many dear friends and extended family. Iraq will always be a significant part of who I am.

I used to live in Baghdad, which I described as a small city where I grew up with my family. In Baghdad, my sisters and brother were students, and I enjoyed a normal life going to school and practicing soccer after classes. We had soccer championships with friends, and the winners would get rewards, which was a fun tradition. Baghdad was home to many of my memories and the place where my father was a soccer superstar. Leaving Baghdad was difficult because it meant leaving behind my friends and the special family gatherings called 'a sima'.

I moved to the United States of America in 2016 with my immediate family, hoping for better education and opportunities. Living here has been challenging, especially with language barriers, adapting to a new culture, and facing racism in public places and school. Over three years, I have learned the language and culture, which has made life easier, though discrimination still exists. I am now a high school student in America and already have my dream job, which gives me hope for the future. The USA has become my new home where I continue to pursue my dreams.
